title: QCalEval: Benchmarking Vision-Language Models for Quantum Calibration Plot Understanding
authors: Shuxiang Cao, Zijian Zhang, Abhishek Agarwal, Grace Bratrud, Niyaz R. Beysengulov, Daniel C. Cole, Alejandro Gómez Frieiro, Elena O. Glen, Hao Hsu, Gang Huang, Raymond Jow, Greshma Shaji, Tom Lubowe, Ligeng Zhu, Luis Mantilla Calderón, Nicola Pancotti, Joel Pendleton, Brandon Severin, Charles Etienne Staub, Sara Sussman, Antti Vepsäläinen, Neel Rajeshbhai Vora, Yilun Xu, Varinia Bernales, Daniel Bowring, Elica Kyoseva, Ivan Rungger, Giulia Semeghini, Sam Stanwyck, Timothy Costa, Alán Aspuru-Guzik, Krysta Svore
abstract: Quantum computing calibration depends on interpreting experimental data, and calibration plots provide the most universal human-readable representation for this task, yet no systematic evaluation exists of how well vision-language models (VLMs) interpret them. We introduce QCalEval, the first VLM benchmark for quantum calibration plots: 243 samples across 87 scenario types from 22 experiment families, spanning superconducting qubits and neutral atoms, evaluated on six question types in both zero-shot and in-context learning settings. The best general-purpose zero-shot model reaches a mean score of 72.3, and many open-weight models degrade under multi-image in-context learning, whereas frontier closed models improve substantially. A supervised fine-tuning ablation at the 9-billion-parameter scale shows that SFT improves zero-shot performance but cannot close the multimodal in-context learning gap. As a reference case study, we release NVIDIA Ising Calibration 1, an open-weight model based on Qwen3.5-35B-A3B that reaches 74.7 zero-shot average score.
summary: This paper introduces QCalEval, the first benchmark designed to test if Vision-Language Models can understand quantum calibration plots. It evaluates models on tasks ranging from simple data extraction to complex scientific diagnosis of superconducting and neutral atom systems. The researchers found that while models are good at seeing features, they struggle with high-level reasoning and often exhibit an 'optimistic bias' toward successful results. The work also presents a fine-tuned model, NVIDIA Ising Calibration 1, which shows improved performance in hardware-specific diagnostics.
main_problem: The lack of a systematic evaluation for how well Vision-Language Models (VLMs) can interpret and diagnose quantum calibration plots, which is essential for automating quantum hardware management.
main_result: The study identifies a 'two-step capability gap' where models detect visual features but fail at scientific reasoning, and an 'MM-ICL gap' where many open-weight models degrade under multi-image in-context learning.
method: The authors introduce QCalEval, a benchmark of 243 samples across 22 experiment families, and evaluate 18 VLMs using zero-shot and in-context learning settings across six task types.
model_or_system: The benchmark covers various quantum platforms, specifically superconducting qubits (e.g., Rabi, Ramsey, DRAG) and neutral atoms (e.g., Rydberg spectroscopy, MOT loading).
key_observables: Peak locations, fringe spacing, contrast, linewidth, clustering, geometric structures (ridges, hotspots, oscillations, decays), and operational statuses (SUCCESS/FAILURE).
important_parameters: Transition frequencies (f01), power regimes, T1/T2* relaxation times, and DRAG coefficients.
main_assumptions: The benchmark uses a mix of simulated and real hardware data; the evaluation assumes that LLM-based judges can accurately assess free-text scientific reasoning.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows diverse calibration plots (1D, 2D, scatter); Figure 2 illustrates the zero-shot vs. in-context learning setup; Figure 3 shows task taxonomy using DRAG calibration; Case studies show failures in readout and Ramsey fringes.
paper_structure: Introduction of the calibration automation problem, presentation of the QCalEval benchmark and task taxonomy, evaluation of various VLMs (closed and open-weight), SFT ablation studies, release of the Ising Calibration 1 model, and discussion of limitations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Representative calibration plots from QCalEval. The benchmark is visually heteroge- neous: it includes 1D line traces with oscillations and decays, 2D spectroscopy maps with ridges and hotspots, scatter or histogram-style readout diagnostics, and image-like measurements of spatial structure. Unlike natural-image benchmarks, the key information is carried by scientific geometry rather than object identity: peak locations, fringe spacing, contrast, linewidth, clustering, and the presence or absence of fitted structure determine whether an experiment is reliable or unreliable.

Figure 2: Task illustration with examples from the qubit spectroscopy experiment. Models are eval- uated under both zero-shot (single plot, no demonstrations) and in-context learning (demonstration examples provided) settings. Each evaluation is an independent conversation with no shared context.

Figure 3: The six question types in QCalEval, illustrated on a DRAG calibration example. Q1 uses a universal prompt across all families; Q2–Q6 prompts are customized per experiment family with family-specific background context, failure mode definitions, and parameter extraction schemas.
